findings.message: `BaselineRustdocCodec` fully deserializes into the current `rustdoc_types::Crate` before checking `format_version`. That means rustdoc JSON from an unsupported schema can fail serde first and be reported as `Json` instead of `UnsupportedFormatVersion` (for example, older format versions before `Crate::target` was added lack the now-required `target` field). Since this codec is meant to reject format mismatches via `UnsupportedFormatVersion`, read/parse just `format_version` first, compare it with `FORMAT_VERSION`, and only then deserialize the full `Crate`.

New external crate prefixes are assigned a placeholder `u32::MAX - 1` during `parse_type_ref_str`, and the returned `Type::ResolvedPath` is never rewritten after the real crate id is registered. The first reference to any unseen crate (for example `serde::Serialize`) will therefore point at an id that does not exist in `external_crates`.

`assign_ids` stores type and trait short names in the same `HashMap<String, Id>` without checking for collisions. If a type and trait share the same short name, one silently overwrites the other, so the later encode pass writes both entries under a single `Id` instead of surfacing the documented `AmbiguousTypeName` error.

`resolved_path_type` builds `Type::ResolvedPath` with an empty `path` string for every impl target. Downstream consumers that render or inspect `Impl.for_` by path will see a blank target type, so inherent and trait impl blocks lose the name of the type they belong to.

`parse_type_ref_str` ignores the `u32` returned by `emit_external_crate` and always leaves the parsed `Type::ResolvedPath` pointing at `u32::MAX - 1`. Any crate-prefixed `TypeRef` therefore becomes a dangling reference instead of the newly registered crate id.

`assign_ids` overwrites `local_name_to_id` on every insert, so a type/trait/function short-name collision silently rebinds earlier entries instead of raising `AmbiguousTypeName` as the port contract promises. That can miswire both `TypeRef` resolution and `TraitImplDeclV2` lookup.

`encode_trait_impl_blocks` emits `Impl` items, but the surrounding struct/enum items only store the inherent impl id (`impls: vec![impl_id]`). The trait-impl ids are never attached anywhere, so consumers walking `Struct::impls` or `Enum::impls` will miss every trait implementation.

`Self` never gets a special case here, so `encode_method_items` will serialize constructors and methods returning `Self` as unresolved markers instead of the owning type. That breaks common APIs that use `Self` in parameter or return position.

Generic-argument handling is lossy here: `AssocType` is rewritten as a plain type argument, while `Const`, `AssocConst`, and `Constraint` are dropped. Valid Rust forms like `Iterator<Item = T>` or `ArrayVec<u8, 32>` will not round-trip to the same `rustdoc_types` shape, so downstream consumers see a different type than the catalogue declared.

External trait impls are encoded with `Path.id = Id(ext_crate_id)`, but `Path.id` is an item id, not a crate id. Because crate ids start at 1, this collides with normal local item ids and can make later `krate.paths` lookups resolve the impl to an unrelated local item instead of treating the trait as external.

Trait methods are emitted with `has_body: true` even though `TraitEntry::methods` are declarations, not implementations. That misclassifies trait-associated functions in the rustdoc graph and can confuse any consumer that uses `has_body` to distinguish signatures from concrete methods.

`Path.id` is an item id in rustdoc JSON, but this branch stores the external crate id (`std_crate_id`) for std-prelude paths and the crate id for crate-prefixed paths. Those ids can collide with real local item ids and cause downstream lookups to resolve the wrong item instead of the referenced external type.

External trait impls are encoded with the sentinel `UNRESOLVED_CRATE_ID` and no matching `paths` entry is created. Downstream consumers recover trait origin through `krate.paths[trait_id]`, so these impls will lose their origin crate and become effectively anonymous.

`TypeKindV2::Struct` is treated as `fields` only: the `pattern` is discarded at the match site, and `encode_struct` always emits `StructKind::Plain`. That means supported catalogue inputs like `CompositePattern::Newtype { .. }` and `TypestateState { .. }` are encoded as empty/plain structs, losing the declared shape of the type.

`resolved_path_type()` builds `Impl.for_` with an empty `path` string for every inherent/trait impl target. Downstream consumers that format or inspect impl targets by path will see a blank type name, so the owning type is effectively lost in the emitted rustdoc graph.

`convert_generic_args()` is lossy for several valid Rust forms: `Const` arguments are downgraded to an unresolved marker, while `AssocConst` and `Constraint` are dropped entirely. A `TypeRef` that uses const generics or higher-order constraints will not round-trip to the same `rustdoc_types` shape.

`encode_trait_impl_blocks` resolves the trait item by short name alone and ignores `origin_crate`. If the crate defines a local item with the same name as an external trait (for example `Display` while also implementing `std::fmt::Display`), this branch will pick the local `Id` and emit an `Impl` that points at the wrong trait, while never registering the external trait path.

The std-prelude allowlist is checked before local catalogue resolution, so a catalogue type or trait named like a prelude item (`Vec`, `Result`, `String`, etc.) is silently encoded as `std::...` instead of the local item. There is no validation in this scope preventing those names, so this produces incorrect `TypeRef` resolution and downstream graph data.

The `syn` parser is only partially implemented, so valid Rust type syntax is silently mangled instead of preserved. `convert_type` falls back to `<unknown_type>` for `dyn Trait`, `impl Trait`, function pointers, parenthesized types, and qualified paths, and `convert_generic_arg` drops `_`, `AssocConst`, and `Constraint` arguments entirely. That means a syntactically valid `TypeRef` can be encoded into a different type with no error, which is a correctness bug for the codec.

`encode_method_items` inserts method `Function` items into `index` but never registers them in `Crate::paths`. The codec therefore emits an incomplete `paths` table for every inherent/trait method, unlike top-level functions and other in-crate items. Any downstream consumer that resolves canonical names or provenance through `paths` will not be able to find methods.

`convert_type_path` treats every multi-segment `syn::TypePath` as an external crate prefix, and it turns any `qself` path into `<qualified_path>`. That mis-encodes valid Rust type refs such as `crate::foo::Bar`, `self::Bar`, `super::Bar`, and associated types like `<T as Trait>::Assoc` / `Iterator::Item` as unresolved markers or fake external crates, which will corrupt `external_crates` and drop type information.

`array_len_to_string` only preserves integer literals; any const-generic array length expression is collapsed to `<const_len>`. `rustdoc_types::Type::Array.len` is supposed to retain the stringified expression, so `[u8; N]`, `[u8; N + 1]`, etc. lose their real length and downstream consumers get incorrect type data.

`dyn Trait` and `impl Trait` bounds bypass all of the identifier-resolution logic used for `Type::Path`: every trait bound is emitted as `Id(u32::MAX)` with a raw path string, so `dyn Clone`, `impl Iterator`, or `impl serde::Serialize` never resolve against the local catalogue, never hit the std-prelude allowlist, and never auto-register external crates.

`Module.items` is built from `state.local_name_to_id.values()` where `local_name_to_id` is a `HashMap`, so the root module's child order is nondeterministic across runs. The same catalogue can therefore serialize with different top-level item ordering, which is flaky for downstream diffs/snapshots.

Const-expression rendering is lossy here: any cast expression is rewritten as `as _`, so valid inputs like `[u8; N as usize]` or `ArrayVec<T, N as usize>` do not round-trip and downstream consumers lose the actual target type.

`CatalogueToExtendedCrateCodec::encode` is a new public API, but this change only adds happy-path coverage. The two error exits (`AmbiguousIdentifier` and invalid `TypeRef`) are not exercised by direct codec tests, so regressions in collision detection or error mapping can slip through.

`STD_PRELUDE_TYPES` are converted to `std::{name}` and tagged with the unresolved sentinel id in both the type-path and trait-bound branches, but many listed entries are not actually defined at that path (`String`, `Vec`, `HashMap`, `Arc`, `Clone`, etc.). That means valid standard-library references are emitted as incorrect paths and become indistinguishable from truly unresolved types.

Associated const generic constraints are serialized with `syn_expr_to_string`, which collapses any expression beyond a literal or simple path to `"<const_expr>"`. Valid inputs such as `Trait<N = A + 1>` or `Trait<LEN = N as usize>` therefore lose their actual value during encoding.

External trait impls are indexed with `path = [origin_crate, trait_name]`, so a `std::fmt::Display` impl becomes `std::Display`. That does not match the canonical def-paths used elsewhere (`type_ref_parser` canonicalizes `Display` to `std::fmt::Display`), so downstream trait-origin lookup will miss or alias std traits.

When `origin_crate` matches the current crate but `local_name_to_id` has no entry, the code allocates a fresh id and never inserts a corresponding trait item or `ItemSummary`. The resulting impl points at a dangling trait reference instead of failing fast.

Bare function pointer parsing drops the source ABI and HRTB lifetimes: `TypeBareFn.abi` is hardcoded to `Abi::Rust`, and `bare_fn.lifetimes` is ignored, so valid `extern "C" fn` / `for<'a> fn` TypeRef strings are misrepresented in the output type graph.

Generic associated-item metadata is lost during parsing. The `Constraint` variant is discarded, and both `AssocType`/`AssocConst` always set `args: None`, so valid forms like `Iterator<Item: Clone>` or `Trait<Assoc<'a> = T>` lose information instead of being encoded into `AssocItemConstraint` data.

`encode_function` drops the crate component from `FunctionPath`: `register_path` always uses `self.crate_name` and `crate_id: 0`, so any cross-workspace function key (`FunctionPath::crate_name`) will be flattened into the current crate and serialized under the wrong path. This should either validate `fn_path.crate_name == doc.crate_name` or preserve the embedded crate name when building `Crate::paths`.

Free-function paths are inserted into `local_name_to_id`, and that map is later used by `resolve_local` for single-segment `TypeRef`s. A root-level function named like a type or prelude item (for example `String` or `Clone`) can therefore be selected as the resolution target, producing a `Path.id` that points at a function item instead of a type/trait. The same shared map also turns otherwise valid type/function name sharing into false `AmbiguousIdentifier` errors.

`Self` is special-cased before the local-name lookup, so a valid catalogue declaration named `Self` can never be referenced: every `TypeRef` with that spelling is forced to the `Id(0)` self placeholder. Since the identifier layer does not reject `Self`, this silently mis-encodes valid input.

Cross-workspace functions are still stamped with `crate_id: 0` when the `Function` item is inserted. `register_path_for_crate(...)` records the right external crate in `paths`, but `Item::crate_id` remains local, so consumers that filter on `crate_id` will misclassify external functions as part of the current crate.

The `std` trait path fallback guesses `std::{name}` for any trait not in `std_canonical_path`'s hardcoded table. That produces wrong definition paths for valid std traits outside the list, such as `std::error::Error` or `std::io::Read`, which then breaks trait-origin resolution and any downstream path comparisons.

`TypeRef` values that are explicitly resolved as std-prelude or crate-prefixed external types are encoded with `Id(UNRESOLVED_CRATE_ID)`, the same marker used for genuinely unknown identifiers. ADR D11/CN-10 says std allowlist and crate-prefixed refs are resolved and must not become Phase 1 unresolved-type errors; only allowlist-outside unknowns should. Because these resolved externals have no distinct id or `paths` entry tying them to `external_crates`, downstream closed-world validation cannot reliably distinguish valid `std::...`/`domain_core::...` references from typos without string-special-casing. Allocate/link a synthetic external item id with a `paths` summary, or use a separate unresolved representation.

`resolve_external_type_ids` only rewrites generic arguments when the `ResolvedPath` itself still has `Id(UNRESOLVED_CRATE_ID)`. For a local generic such as `Wrapper<serde::Value>`, the outer `Wrapper` resolves to a local id, so the match falls through and the nested external `serde::Value` keeps the unresolved sentinel even though `serde` was registered. Phase 1 will then reject a valid crate-prefixed TypeRef as undeclared. Recurse into `p.args` for every `ResolvedPath`, not only unresolved ones.

The catch-all arm explicitly passes through `DynTrait` and `FunctionPointer`, but the parser can produce external paths inside both forms, for example `dyn Clone`, `dyn serde::Serialize`, or `fn(serde::Value) -> String`. Those nested paths remain at `Id(UNRESOLVED_CRATE_ID)` after external-crate registration, so closed-world validation will treat valid std/external references as unresolved. Add recursive fix-up for `Type::DynTrait` and `Type::FunctionPointer` contents.

`function_info_to_method_decl()` can now return `None`, but `outgoing` is still computed from `inherent_methods` before filtering. If a method is skipped, the node keeps its typestate transition targets even though the method no longer exists in `methods()`, which makes the graph internally inconsistent. Build `outgoing` from the same filtered set or skip the whole method before both collections.

`extract_params()` silently drops any parameter whose name or formatted type fails the newtype validation. Because `export()` already returns `Result`, this turns invalid rustdoc input into a truncated `SchemaExport` instead of an error, so downstream consumers lose method parameters without any signal. Propagate a codec error instead of `filter_map`-skipping.

`function_info_to_method_decl` is no longer a lossless adapter: it always drops `FunctionInfo::docs` and silently collapses any receiver string that fails `SelfReceiver` parsing to `None`, while the surrounding code still uses `FunctionInfo::has_self_receiver()` to compute outgoing transitions. That means a `TypeGraph` built from non-canonical input can end up with method signatures, docs, and transition edges that disagree, and even on valid input every method doc is discarded before the baseline/render path sees it. `schema_export.rs` already populates these docs upstream, so this loses data that the new `MethodDeclaration` model can carry.

Phase 2 structural equality is comparing graph-local IDs instead of item shape for structs/enums, and traits are reduced to an item-count check. Two identical APIs encoded with different rustdoc IDs will therefore be classified as mismatches, which breaks the core signal table.

Phase 1 copies A child items into S using their original IDs and silently skips any occupied slot. Because A and B use independent ID spaces, nested Add/Modify items can alias unrelated B items or drop their children entirely, corrupting S before Phase 1.5/2 run.

`build_external_crates_for_scope` rebuilds `external_crates` with fresh IDs but leaves the copied `ItemSummary.crate_id` values untouched. The returned S/D crate graphs are internally inconsistent, so any consumer that resolves `paths` through `crate_id` will look up the wrong external crate.

`collect_child_ids` stops at `Enum` items and never descends into `ItemEnum::Variant` payloads, so tuple/struct variant fields are never copied/remapped into S. That leaves enum payload items missing or stale after Add/Modify/Delete and can produce malformed graphs or bogus dangling-id behavior.

The type walker only handles a small subset of `rustdoc_types::Type` variants. `FunctionPointer`, `QualifiedPath`, `ImplTrait`, `DynTrait`, and `GenericArgs::Parenthesized` are effectively ignored, so unresolved refs in those shapes bypass Phase 1 validation and structurally different APIs can collapse to the same `_` rendering in Phase 2.

`build_variant_shape_map` drops `None` slots from `VariantKind::Tuple(Vec<Option<Id>>)`. A variant with stripped/hidden fields can therefore normalize to the same `tuple(...)` string as a shorter variant, which makes Phase 2 report a match for unequal enum shapes.

`fn_sigs_structurally_equal` ignores `FunctionSignature.is_c_variadic` and all `FunctionHeader` fields except `is_async`. Variadic, `extern`, `const`, or `unsafe` functions can therefore be classified as structurally equal to a plain function with the same parameter/return types.

P1: Phase 1 allocates new Ids from `1` without reserving any Ids already present in `B`. Because B items are inserted into `s_index` before any A-side `Add`/`Delete`/`Modify` allocation, the first fresh allocation will reuse an existing rustdoc id and overwrite the baseline entry in `s_index`/`d_index`. In practice this corrupts S for any non-empty baseline crate.

P1: Qualified-path trait references are never resolved or checked for dangling ids. `type_has_unresolved_marker`, `resolve_type`, and `collect_ids_from_type` recurse into `Type::QualifiedPath.self_type` and its generic args, but they ignore `trait_.id` entirely. That lets unresolved or deleted trait refs in projections like `<T as Trait>::Assoc` survive Phase 1.5/1.6 without an error.

P1: `insert_s_type` hardcodes `ItemSummary.kind` to `ItemKind::Struct` for every inserted type/trait item. Enums, traits, and type aliases therefore get emitted with incorrect `Crate::paths` metadata, which makes the returned S/D crates structurally wrong for downstream consumers that inspect path kinds.

Deleting or modifying a trait only moves the trait root; its copied child items stay in `s_index`. Because phase 2 scans the whole crate for `Function` and `TypeAlias` identities, stale trait methods/associated types can still be emitted as standalone signals after the trait was removed or replaced.

`GenericArgs::AngleBracketed.constraints` are dropped here, and the same omission appears in the resolver/ID collector/formatter paths. Constrained types like `Iterator<Item = Foo>` can therefore compare equal when the associated binding changes, and unresolved or dangling refs inside those constraints are never validated.

`Type::FunctionPointer` rendering keeps `is_unsafe` but drops the ABI, so `extern \"C\" fn` and plain `fn` fields/type aliases collapse to the same structural string. That can hide real API changes in phase 2.

Phase 1 never traverses `Type::ImplTrait` or `Type::DynTrait` when scanning for unresolved markers or dangling ids. As a result, a deleted or unresolved trait bound inside `impl Trait` / `dyn Trait` can slip through validation and be treated as a valid match in Phase 2.

The unresolved-marker check only rejects `Id(UNRESOLVED_CRATE_ID)` when the path has no `::`. The A codec emits the same sentinel shape for unresolved `crate::`, `self::`, and `super::` references, so those unresolved local paths bypass Phase 1.5 instead of raising `UnresolvedTypeRef`.

`fn_sigs_structurally_equal` compares only the rendered inputs/output plus header flags; it never checks `Function::generics` or where-clause metadata. A generic-constraint change can therefore be classified as a match and reported as Blue/Skip when it should be a structural mismatch.

`collect_ids_from_type` pushes every `ResolvedPath` id unconditionally, so external markers like `std::vec::Vec` stay as `Id(UNRESOLVED_CRATE_ID)`. The Phase 1.6 loop then treats that marker as a dangling reference, which means any surviving item that uses an external crate type will fail with `DanglingId` even though those references are expected to remain unresolved.

This identity map includes every `ItemEnum::Function`, which in rustdoc also covers methods inside `trait`/`impl` blocks. Because Phase 1 does not preserve child `paths` entries when copying those items into S, the fallback here collapses pathless methods to bare names, so unrelated methods like `new` or `read` can overwrite each other and produce spurious function signals. Restrict this to free functions or keep a disambiguating identity for associated methods.

P1: Structural equality is too weak for generic types. `TypeAlias` compares only the underlying target type, `Struct`/`Enum` comparisons ignore `Generics` completely, and `generics_structurally_equal` collapses every non-lifetime parameter to a count. That means changes like `T` vs `const N: usize`, renamed/added type params, or altered generic bounds can be reported as a match/skip even though the API shape changed.

P1: Phase 1 validation does not traverse `Impl` items. `collect_type_refs_from_item` and `resolve_unresolved_in_item` only inspect `StructField`, `TypeAlias`, and `Function`, so an impl block that still references a deleted trait/type survives Phase 1 without `UnresolvedTypeRef` or `DanglingId`. Deleting a trait while a surviving type still has an impl for it is therefore a false negative.

Phase 1 only walks `Type` nodes from fields/type aliases/functions/impls. It never inspects `Generics` on structs, enums, traits, or functions, so unresolved markers and deleted-item references inside bounds/where-clauses are never rejected. That leaves invalid A items able to pass Phase 1 and also bypass the Phase 1.6 dangling-id check.

Phase 2 structural comparison drops important generic payloads: `ImplTrait`/`DynTrait` keep only trait short names, `Type::QualifiedPath` ignores `trait_.args`, and `GenericArgs::Constraint(_)` is discarded entirely. Distinct signatures such as `impl Iterator<Item = u8>` vs `impl Iterator<Item = u16>` can therefore compare equal and produce the wrong signal.

Phase 1 detects unresolved markers in struct/enum/trait/function/impl generics, but `resolve_unresolved_in_item` never walks those generics, and `resolve_type` has no arms for `ImplTrait` or `DynTrait`. Unresolved references inside generic bounds/defaults or `impl Trait`/`dyn Trait` types will therefore survive Phase 1.5 instead of raising `UnresolvedTypeRef`.

`build_external_crate_names_from_type` does not inspect the `trait_` path itself for `Type::QualifiedPath`, and it ignores `Type::ImplTrait` entirely. If an item only references a foreign crate through a projection or trait bound, the per-scope `external_crates` set can be incomplete and `crate_id` patching can stay wrong.

`traits_structurally_equal` delegates to `build_trait_method_map`, but that map drops method generics and where-clauses. Two traits whose methods differ only by generic parameters or bounds can compare equal and be reported as matching in Phase 2.

P1: Both identity builders scan every entry in `krate.index` without checking `item.crate_id == 0`, so cross-crate items that the codec stores with non-zero crate ids will be treated as local API and fed into Phase 1/2. That can produce false signals for foreign functions/types and makes S/D include items that are not part of the crate under review.

P1: `generics_structurally_equal` only compares the count/kind of generic params plus where predicates; it drops each type/const param’s bound/default/type entirely. As a result, signatures like `fn f<T: Copy>()` vs `fn f<T: Clone>()` or `const N: usize` vs `const N: u64` compare equal, so real API changes can be misclassified as unchanged.

P1: Trait structural comparison collapses every non-function associated item to `""`, so associated types/consts are only checked for presence, not shape. A trait whose associated type default or bounds change will still compare equal, which suppresses a breaking-change signal.

P1: Phase 1 does not fully walk the `rustdoc_types::Item`/`GenericArgs` surface. `collect_type_refs_from_item` and `resolve_unresolved_in_item` ignore `ItemEnum::AssocType` / `ItemEnum::AssocConst`, and `resolve_generic_args` / `collect_ids_from_generic_args` only handle `AssocItemConstraintKind::Equality`, not `Constraint(bounds)`. That means unresolved markers or deleted-item `Id`s embedded in trait associated types/consts or generic constraints can survive Phase 1 and reach Phase 2, which violates the contract that all unresolved refs and dangling ids are rejected early.

P1: `Impl` items are never included in the identity maps or Phase 2 evaluation. `build_type_trait_identity_map` / `build_function_identity_map` only cover types, traits, and free functions, and the final `C \ (S ∪ D)` pass only checks `c_types` and `c_fns`, so undeclared implementations in C can never produce the required `CMinusSUnionD` red signal.

P1: External crate metadata is not reconstructed for A-sourced refs. `collect_external_crate_names_from_type()` only records crates when `p.id != UNRESOLVED_CRATE_ID`, but `type_ref_parser` uses that sentinel for every external path (including `std::...` and `serde::...`). As a result, S/D can be built with incomplete `external_crates` and unpatched `ItemSummary.crate_id` values whenever the catalogue introduces a new external dependency. See [signal_evaluator_v2.rs:1768](file:///home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/libs/infrastructure/src/tddd/signal_evaluator_v2.rs#L1768).

P1: Impl handling is incomplete and will misclassify signals. `phase2_evaluate()` includes impls in the report, but `items_structurally_equal()` has no `ItemEnum::Impl` arm, so every S∩C impl is treated as a mismatch even when identical. On top of that, `build_impl_identity_map()` keys impls by short type/trait name only, so distinct impls can overwrite each other. See [signal_evaluator_v2.rs:152](file:///home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/libs/infrastructure/src/tddd/signal_evaluator_v2.rs#L152) and [signal_evaluator_v2.rs:2088](file:///home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/libs/infrastructure/src/tddd/signal_evaluator_v2.rs#L2088).

P1: `args_have_unresolved_marker()` ignores `AssocItemConstraintKind::Constraint(bounds)` inside angle-bracketed generic args. That means unresolved placeholders nested in associated-item constraint bounds never enter Phase 1.5, and Phase 1.6 cannot catch them because the sentinel id is explicitly skipped there. See [signal_evaluator_v2.rs:982](file:///home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/libs/infrastructure/src/tddd/signal_evaluator_v2.rs#L982).

Phase 1.6 rejects any referenced `Id` that is not in `s_index` or `s_paths`, but `s_paths` only stores the top-level local items inserted by `insert_s_type`/`insert_s_fn`. Rustdoc external references still use real `ResolvedPath` ids from `b.paths`/`external_crates`, so ordinary std/external types in fields, bounds, or signatures will now trip `Phase1Error::DanglingId` even though they are valid.

Impl identity is collapsed too aggressively: `build_impl_identity_map` keys only on `format_type(&impl_.for_)` plus the trait short name, and `phase2_evaluate` uses that key for S/D/C matching. Distinct impls that differ by trait origin or trait arguments can overwrite each other, so one impl can disappear from the report entirely instead of being evaluated separately.

Phase 1 never seeds or deletes `ItemEnum::Impl` items: S/D only receive types/traits and free functions, and `collect_child_ids` never traverses the `Struct::impls` links that point to impl blocks. As a result, `build_impl_identity_map` in Phase 2 will see empty S/D impl sets and every impl in C will fall into `CMinusSUnionD`, even when it is declared in A/B.

`build_function_identity_map` treats every local `ItemEnum::Function` with a `paths` entry as a free function, but it never filters out ids that belong to trait or impl method lists. If a method carries a rustdoc path, it will be counted as a standalone function signal in addition to its containing trait/impl, causing duplicate or misplaced Phase 2 results.

P1: Deleting a type drops all of its child impl blocks from `S`, but never transfers those impls into `D`. Because phase 2 only evaluates impls by scanning `D`, any trait impl attached to a deleted type disappears from `DIntersectC` / `DMinusC` entirely instead of being reported as deleted.

P1: Generic-bound ordering is treated as significant in structural equality. `format_where_predicate()` feeds `format_generic_bounds()` directly into the comparison key, so semantically equivalent bounds such as `where T: A + B` vs `where T: B + A` compare unequal and will produce false phase-2 mismatches.

Deleting a type/trait with impl blocks leaves the impl descendants behind. `move_impl_children_to_d` removes the `Impl` node before `remove_child_items_from_s` runs, so the recursive purge can no longer reach that impl's methods/associated items. Those orphaned items can survive Phase 1 and be misclassified in Phase 2.

`impl` comparison is reduced to `for_` + trait path only. Combined with the function-identity filter that excludes impl/trait methods, any change inside an impl block (added/removed/changed methods or associated consts/types) is invisible to the evaluator, so real API changes will still look like a match.

The impl branch only compares `for_`, trait path, and the child-item map. It ignores `impl_.generics`, `is_unsafe`, `is_negative`, `blanket_impl`, and `is_synthetic`, so a changed impl header can still be reported as a match. Because `build_impl_identity_map` also admits every local impl block, rustdoc blanket/synthetic/negative impls will be treated as ordinary contract items and can produce spurious `CMinusSUnionD` reds.

Function-pointer types are only walked through `sig.inputs` and `sig.output`. Their `generic_params` are never resolved, never checked for dangling ids, and never included in `format_type`, so generic or higher-ranked fn-pointer signatures can evade Phase 1 validation and compare equal in Phase 2.

Using only the short name as the identity key means two types/traits with the same name in different modules overwrite each other in the Phase 1 maps. That can silently drop one item from S/D and can also resolve later `crate::Foo` references to the wrong `Foo` during Phase 1.5.

Trait supertrait bounds are never carried through this pipeline: `collect_type_refs_from_item` ignores `Trait.bounds`, `resolve_unresolved_in_item` never rewrites them, and `traits_structurally_equal` does not compare them. As a result, deleted/renamed supertraits can slip past Phase 1.6 and real trait API changes in `bounds` will be reported as unchanged.

The dangling-id check only rejects references that are present in `b.paths` with `crate_id == 0`; any other unknown `Id` is explicitly accepted as "valid". That means a malformed local reference that is neither an S item, a B child item, nor an external-crate item will survive Phase 1.6 instead of failing closed.

Phase 2 is emitting type/trait identities as full module paths here, but the `SignalRegion`/`ThreeWaySignal` contract is short-name based for types and traits. Because `phase2_evaluate` forwards this key verbatim, the report will no longer match downstream consumers that key signals by short name.

`impl_.for_` is still formatted with `format_type`, which strips module paths. Two impl blocks for distinct `Foo` types in different modules collapse to the same `Foo: Trait` key, and one overwrites the other in the map, so one impl signal is silently dropped.

Phase 1.6 only exempts ids found in `b.paths` with `crate_id != 0`, so A-sourced external types that the codec rewrites to synthetic ids are still treated as dangling and rejected. Legitimate references like `std`/`serde` will trip `DanglingId` even though they are valid external dependencies.

`build_impl_identity_map` keys impls by `format_type(&impl_.for_)`, which strips module paths. Two distinct `Foo` types in different modules that implement the same trait will collide and one impl will overwrite the other, so Phase 2 silently loses signals for same-named types.

Structural comparison is order-sensitive for associated-type/const constraints inside generic args. `format_generic_args` preserves `constraints` in input order, but `format_type` is the equality key, so equivalent paths with reordered constraints can compare unequal and produce false mismatch signals.

P1: `format_generic_bounds` / `build_generics_fingerprint` drop `GenericBound::TraitBound.modifier`, so `T: Sized` and `T: ?Sized` normalize to the same fingerprint. That feeds `generics_structurally_equal`, so real API changes in generic bounds can be reported as matches. See [format.rs](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/libs/infrastructure/src/tddd/signal_evaluator_v2/format.rs#L231) and [structural_eq.rs](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/libs/infrastructure/src/tddd/signal_evaluator_v2/structural_eq.rs#L428).

P1: `format_type` for `Type::FunctionPointer` omits `FunctionHeader.is_const` and `is_async`; only ABI/unsafe/variadic are encoded. That collapses distinct function-pointer types into the same structural key, so field and alias comparisons can miss changes. See [format.rs](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/libs/infrastructure/src/tddd/signal_evaluator_v2/format.rs#L122).

Deleting a parent item transfers only the `Impl` node into `D`, not its subtree, and the impl's `for_` still points at the old S-side id. `D.index` therefore contains dangling impl references and loses the method/assoc-item data needed to keep the returned crate graph internally consistent.

`build_impl_identity_map` uses `format_type(&impl_.for_)` plus the raw trait path string as the key. That collapses distinct impls for same-named types in different modules (`a::Foo` vs `b::Foo` both become `Foo`) and also fails to match semantically identical local trait paths when A emits `crate::...` but rustdoc emits `my_crate::...`. The result is silent impl loss or mismatched Phase 2 signals.

Local unresolved-path resolution only strips a single `crate::`/`self::`/`super::` prefix and then looks up the remaining string in the short-name map. For nested paths like `crate::nested::User`, the lookup becomes `nested::User`, which can never resolve even when `User` exists in S. Valid A-sourced references in nested modules will therefore be rejected as `UnresolvedTypeRef`.

Impl structural comparison uses raw trait-path string equality, so local impls that normalize to the same identity key (`crate::MyTrait` vs `my_crate::MyTrait`) still compare unequal and Phase 2 will report false mismatches for matching impls.

Impl trait-path resolution only strips one leading prefix before lookup; a valid nested local path like `crate::nested::User` becomes `nested::User`, which never matches the short-name map and raises `UnresolvedTypeRef` for valid A items.

Deleted types are moved to D with a fresh Id, but transferred impl blocks are inserted unchanged. Their `for_` still points at the old S/B Id, so D ends up with dangling impl references instead of a self-consistent graph.

Impl identity is still normalized to the last path segment for every trait, so distinct traits with the same short name collide. That means Phase 2 can drop or misclassify impl signals when a crate uses `serde::Serialize` and `other::Serialize`-style names; `items_structurally_equal` strips trait paths the same way, so the collision is not confined to the map builder.

Plain structs and struct variants ignore the `has_stripped_fields` marker, so a rustdoc shape with hidden fields can compare equal to a fully visible empty/unit shape. That turns truncated docs into false matches instead of conservative mismatches.

`format_type` drops important qualifiers from composite types. `DynTrait.lifetime` is never rendered, and function-pointer formatting omits `is_const`, so distinct types like `dyn Foo + 'a` vs `dyn Foo + 'static` or `const fn(...)` vs `fn(...)` collapse to the same string and can produce false structural matches.

Local impls from rustdoc will not match codec impls here. `normalize_impl_trait_path()` only strips `crate::`/`self::`/`super::` prefixes, so `my_crate::Trait` is preserved verbatim even though the comments and Phase 2 contract say it should normalize to the same short name as `MyTrait`. That makes `build_impl_identity_map()` and the impl branch in `items_structurally_equal()` split the same impl into different keys/signatures, producing spurious mismatch/undeclared-impl signals for local impls.

`Trait::implementations` is never treated as a child subtree. `collect_child_ids()` only returns `t.items` for `ItemEnum::Trait`, so deleting a trait moves the trait item and its methods but leaves all of its impl blocks in S. Those impls still carry `trait_.id` pointing at the old B-side trait, so Phase 1.6 will report `DanglingId` instead of completing a trait delete. Include the trait implementation ids in the child walk or transfer them when moving a trait to D.

`Type::Pat` is unsupported throughout the new type walkers. It falls through `type_has_unresolved_marker()`/`collect_ids_from_type()`/`resolve_type()` and `format_type()` collapses it to `_`. Any pattern-typed field or signature can therefore skip Phase 1.5/1.6 validation, and distinct pattern types can compare equal in Phase 2. This needs an explicit `Type::Pat` branch that recurses into the underlying type/pattern payload.

`ImplTrait`/`DynTrait` formatting is order-sensitive here, unlike `format_generic_bounds`, which sorts equivalent bounds. That means semantically equivalent signatures such as `impl A + B` vs `impl B + A` (and the analogous `dyn` cases) can compare unequal in Phase 2, producing spurious mismatch signals.

Normalizing impl trait paths to the last segment collapses distinct external traits with the same short name, and the same helper is reused by structural equality. A crate that implements both `serde::Serialize` and `other::Serialize` for the same type will key both impls as `Type: Serialize`, so one overwrites the other and Phase 2 can miss or misattribute a signal.

B-seeded impl blocks are copied into S with their original `impl.for_.id` unchanged. Phase 1.6 then sees that old B parent id as dangling because the parent was reinserted under a fresh S id, so any baseline type/trait with impls will fail Phase 1 with `DanglingId`.

Phase 2 builds S impl identities with `build_impl_identity_map(s_krate, "")`, but S contains rustdoc-style B impls whose local trait paths look like `my_crate::Trait`. With an empty crate name those paths are kept verbatim instead of normalized, so valid S impls never match C/D keys and are reported as false `SMinusC`/`CMinusSUnionD` signals.

Impl-trait identity keys are built with `format!("{}{}", normalized_trait_path, format_generic_args(args))`, so there is no delimiter between the path and its generic args. That lets distinct impls collide, e.g. `Foo` + `Bar` and `FooBar` both normalize to the same key, and `structural_eq.rs` repeats the same concatenation when comparing impls.

`format_type`/`format_generic_bounds` drop non-trait bounds (`GenericBound::Outlives` and `GenericBound::Use`) from `impl Trait` and generic where-clauses, so structurally different APIs can compare equal. For example, `impl Copy + 'a` and `impl Copy` both stringify to `impl Copy`, and the same omission feeds `generics_structurally_equal` via the shared helpers.

`resolve_type` rewrites function-pointer inputs and outputs but leaves `fp.generic_params` unchanged. `collect_refs.rs` already scans those HRTB params for unresolved markers and dangling ids, so a local `UNRESOLVED_CRATE_ID` hidden in a `for<'a>` bound survives Phase 1.5 and is never rejected in Phase 1.6.

The crate-name collector does not recurse through `Type::RawPointer` or `Type::Pat`, so an external dependency that only appears under `*const/*mut` types or pattern types is omitted from the per-scope `external_crates` map and never gets its `ItemSummary.crate_id` renumbered.

`Type::FunctionPointer` is only scanned for its `sig.inputs`/`sig.output`; its `generic_params` are skipped entirely. That means external types referenced in higher-ranked function-pointer bounds/defaults are also invisible to external-crate collection, leaving the scope metadata incomplete.

A-sourced impl blocks keep their original `impl.for_.id`: `insert_a_item_tree_into_s` remaps only child-item ids, and `remap_child_ids_in_item` explicitly skips `Type::ResolvedPath`. For any added/modified type that has impls, the impl self-type still points at the old A id, so Phase 1 can either trip a false dangling-id error or silently bind the impl to an unrelated B item that reuses that numeric id.

`patch_impl_for_ids` rewrites every copied impl to the parent item's id, but rustdoc trait `implementations` do not use `for_` as the parent relation. For trait impls the relevant parent reference is `impl.trait_`, while `for_` is still the implementing type. This corrupts trait-impl identity during baseline seeding and trait deletion, leaving `trait_.id` stale and making Phase 2 classify those impls against the wrong key.

`build_type_trait_identity_map` and `build_impl_identity_map` use plain `BTreeMap::insert`, so the last item with a given short name silently wins. Because `krate.index` iteration order is hash-based, a crate with two `User` types (or two `User: Trait` impls) in different modules will nondeterministically drop one item and its signals instead of rejecting the ambiguity.

Higher-ranked trait-bound `generic_params` are never traversed. `resolve_generic_bound` preserves them unchanged, and the Phase 1.5/1.6 scanners plus Phase 2 formatting/equality never recurse into them either, so unresolved or dangling ids inside `for<'a, T: ...>`-style bounds are missed and distinct `impl Trait` / `dyn Trait` shapes can compare equal.

Phase 1 never traverses or rewrites `GenericBound::TraitBound.generic_params`: `collect_type_refs_from_generics` drops them, and `resolve_generic_bound` returns them unchanged. That means HRTB bounds like `for<'a, T: crate::Foo>` can keep unresolved `Id(UNRESOLVED_CRATE_ID)` markers without ever triggering `UnresolvedTypeRef`, so invalid refs can survive Phase 1.5/1.6 silently.

Phase 2 generic comparison is too lossy: `build_generics_fingerprint` and `generics_structurally_equal` only record trait-path strings, and `format_generic_bounds` also omits nested `generic_params`. As a result, bounds that differ only by lifetime/HRTB detail (`T: 'a`, `T: use<'a>`, `for<'a>` vs `for<'b>`) compare equal, so structurally changed items can be reported as matches.

Inherent `impl` blocks are dropped from Phase 2 entirely. `build_impl_identity_map` skips every `Impl` with `trait_ == None`, so undeclared inherent impls in `C` never reach `CMinusSUnionD`, and any add/modify/delete on inherent impls is invisible to the evaluator.

Phase 1 remaps item ids for copied subtrees, but `remap_child_ids_in_item` only rewrites structural child-id lists. It never rewrites embedded `Type::ResolvedPath` ids, so any recursive or sibling reference whose target id is reassigned in S will still point at the old id and be rejected later as a false `DanglingId`.

`Type::ImplTrait`/`Type::DynTrait` keep `generic_params` unchanged while only rewriting the outer trait path. Any unresolved local id nested in those binders survives Phase 1.5, and Phase 1.6 will not catch it because the sentinel `UNRESOLVED_CRATE_ID` is skipped when collecting dangling ids.

Trait-bound normalization drops the `modifier` field and only keeps the trait path/args. That collapses distinct bounds like `T: Sized` vs `T: ?Sized` in Phase 2; the same lossiness also shows up in the generic fingerprints used by `generics_eq.rs`, so real signature changes can still be reported as matches.

Phase 1 still ignores `WherePredicate::BoundPredicate.generic_params` when scanning and resolving type refs. `collect_type_refs_from_generics` drops those binder params entirely, and `resolve_generics` in `resolve_type.rs` copies them through unchanged, so a `where for<'a, T: Foo>` binder can keep unresolved or deleted-item references hidden from Phase 1. That means S can pass validation with dangling refs still embedded in the where-clause HRTB.

Phase 2 canonicalization is lossy for higher-ranked binders: `format_type` renders `Type::FunctionPointer` with only lifetime binders, `Type::ImplTrait` / `Type::DynTrait` drop `generic_params` entirely, and `format_where_predicate` strips `WherePredicate::BoundPredicate.generic_params`. As a result, two signatures that differ only in HRTB binder bounds or const/type params can compare equal and be classified as a match when they should mismatch.

`insert_b_item_tree_into_s()` reassigns a fresh S id to every baseline root item, but it only patches impl parent pointers. It never rewrites `Type::ResolvedPath.id` values that point at other top-level B items, so ordinary baseline cross-references like `Order.user_id -> UserId` keep the old B id and are later reported as `DanglingId` even when the target is still present in S. That makes Phase 1 reject valid crates with internal type references.

Structural equality is not alpha-equivalent: `format_type()` renders `Type::Generic(name)` verbatim, and the same stringification is used by the function/trait/impl comparators. Renaming generic parameters (`T` -> `U`) in an otherwise identical item will therefore produce a false mismatch signal, contrary to the module docs that say parameter binding names are ignored.

P1: `generics_structurally_equal` and the sibling fingerprint builder both drop non-trait bounds on type parameters (`GenericBound::Outlives` / `GenericBound::Use`). `format.rs` explicitly treats those bounds as part of the rendered signature, so Phase 2 can now report a `Match`/`Skip` for APIs whose only change is a lifetime or capture bound on a generic parameter. See [`generics_eq.rs`](//home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/libs/infrastructure/src/tddd/signal_evaluator_v2/generics_eq.rs#L112) and [`generics_eq.rs`](//home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/libs/infrastructure/src/tddd/signal_evaluator_v2/generics_eq.rs#L214), contrasted with [`format.rs`](//home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/libs/infrastructure/src/tddd/signal_evaluator_v2/format.rs#L119).

P1: Phase 1 validation does not recurse into higher-ranked trait-bound binder type parameters inside `GenericBound::TraitBound`. The resolver does recurse there, so unresolved or dangling ids nested in `for<T: ...>`-style binders can slip through Phase 1.5 / 1.6 undetected. The gap is in [`collect_refs.rs`](//home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/libs/infrastructure/src/tddd/signal_evaluator_v2/collect_refs.rs#L47), compared with the recursive handling in [`resolve_type.rs`](//home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/libs/infrastructure/src/tddd/signal_evaluator_v2/resolve_type.rs#L55).

Phase 1.6 treats any `Id` found in `b.index` as a valid child-item reference, so references to deleted child items (for example an associated type or struct field removed with its parent) are never reported as `DanglingId`. That lets stale ids survive into Phase 2.

`build_trait_method_map` omits `FunctionHeader::abi` when comparing trait/impl methods, so two methods with the same name and parameter/return types but different ABI compare equal. Phase 2 can therefore classify a real mismatch as a match.

P1: the `Modify` path inserts a remapped A subtree but never patches direct `impl` children back to the preserved S-side parent id. `remap_and_copy_a_children_to_s()` only remaps child lists and returns the root item; it does not call `patch_impl_for_ids()` / `patch_impl_trait_ids()`. Any modified struct/trait with impl blocks will keep `impl.for_.id` or `impl.trait_.id` pointing at the old A-side id, which can trip Phase 1.6 dangling-id validation or mis-key Phase 2 impl matching. The `Add` path already does the patching, so `Modify` needs the same step.

`resolve_type` has no `Type::Pat` arm, so unresolved markers nested inside pattern types survive Phase 1.5 unchanged. The item is only scanned once before resolution, so that placeholder can bypass the intended `UnresolvedTypeRef` failure path and reach Phase 2 still unresolved.

`format_hrtb_type_params` only formats the first layer of HRTB binders. Any trait bound nested inside a binder's own `generic_params` is dropped, so `generics_structurally_equal` and `build_generics_fingerprint` treat distinct nested `for<...>` bounds as equal and can emit false matches.

`build_external_crates_for_scope` does not recurse into nested HRTB binders when harvesting external crate names. If an external type appears only inside a nested `for<...>` bound, that crate is omitted from the scope's `external_crates` map and the patched `ItemSummary.crate_id` data becomes incomplete.

P1: The evaluator silently collapses same-named items, so only one `Foo` survives when a crate has `Foo` in multiple modules. `build_type_trait_identity_map` and the Phase 1 name maps are keyed by short name only, and the `or_insert`/`get` path means later collisions are dropped or resolved to the wrong `Id`; `build_impl_identity_map` has the same problem for local trait impls. That can misclassify Add/Modify/Delete/Reference actions and lose Phase 2 signals for crates that legitimately contain same-name types/traits in different modules.

P1: Traversal drops `GenericBound::TraitBound.generic_params` in the unresolved/dangling-reference and external-crate collectors, so anything nested under an HRTB binder is invisible to Phase 1.5/1.6 and to `external_crates` synthesis. In `phase1/builder.rs`, Phase 1.5 only runs `resolve_unresolved_in_item` on items flagged by `item_has_unresolved_marker`; but `collect_type_refs_from_item` flattens trait/assoc-type bounds to `Type::ResolvedPath` without recursing into their binder params, and `collect_ids_from_generic_args` / `collect_ext_crate_names_from_generic_args` do the same for constraint bounds. That means unresolved markers or dangling `Id`s inside those binders can survive, and external crates referenced only there are not added. See [phase1/builder.rs](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/libs/infrastructure/src/tddd/signal_evaluator_v2/phase1/builder.rs#L230), [collect_refs.rs](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/libs/infrastructure/src/tddd/signal_evaluator_v2/collect_refs.rs#L225), [collect_refs.rs](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/libs/infrastructure/src/tddd/signal_evaluator_v2/collect_refs.rs#L399), and [external_crates.rs](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/libs/infrastructure/src/tddd/signal_evaluator_v2/external_crates.rs#L235).

Trait-path formatting collapses everything to the last path segment in `format_type`, `format_generic_bounds`, and `format_hrtb_type_params`. That makes distinct external traits such as `serde::Serialize` and `other::Serialize` compare equal in Phase 2, so `items_structurally_equal` can miss real mismatches in function signatures, trait bounds, `impl Trait`/`dyn Trait`, and qualified paths.

P1: `collect_type_refs_from_item()` does not recurse into `GenericBound::TraitBound.generic_params` for `ItemEnum::AssocType` bounds. That means associated-type bounds like `type Assoc: for<T: LocalTrait> ...` can hide unresolved markers, dangling ids, and external-crate references from Phase 1.5/1.6 and `build_external_crates_for_scope`, so invalid references in those binders can pass validation silently.

P1: [`format.rs`](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/libs/infrastructure/src/tddd/signal_evaluator_v2/format.rs#L121) collapses every `Type::Generic` to the same placeholder (`"_"`). That destroys which generic parameter a use site refers to, so distinct signatures like `fn f<T, U>(x: T, y: U)` and `fn f<T, U>(x: T, y: T)` both compare equal. The same loss of information flows into struct/enum/type-alias and impl/trait comparisons because they all rely on `format_type`, which can produce false Blue/Match signals for real structural mismatches.

P1: Trait structural comparison ignores `Function.has_body`, so a trait method changing between required (`fn foo(&self);`) and defaulted (`fn foo(&self) { ... }`) still hashes to the same signature and is treated as equal. That can suppress a real API change in Phase 2. The omission is in [`generics_eq.rs`](...) where the `ItemEnum::Function` branch only fingerprints header/signature/generics.

P1: `build_external_crates_for_scope` assigns fresh external crate ids by iterating a `HashSet`, so the order is randomized per process. The same input crate can therefore produce different `external_crates` numbering and different patched `paths.crate_id` values across runs, making the phase-1 output nondeterministic and flaky for any downstream serialization/diffing.

`type_has_unresolved_marker()` does not descend into `Type::QualifiedPath.args`, so unresolved local refs nested in qualified-path generic arguments can bypass Phase 1.5 and survive without being resolved or rejected.

A-sourced child impl blocks are inserted here without any `ItemAction`, so `phase2_evaluate()` falls back to `Reference` for them and skips blue/yellow/red signals for impls under added/modified types or traits.

A-sourced enum variants never rewrite their payload field ids during Phase 1 remapping. `collect_child_ids` treats `Variant` payloads as children, but `remap_child_ids_in_item` leaves `VariantKind::Tuple/Struct` unchanged, so inserted variants still point at the old A-side ids. That leaves stale intra-enum links and makes Phase 2 enum comparison see missing fields instead of the real payload.

A-sourced traits also lose their `implementations` remap. `collect_child_ids` includes `t.implementations`, but `remap_child_ids_in_item` only rewrites `t.items`. The trait item keeps stale impl ids, so later delete/move logic cannot find or transfer the impl blocks and they can remain behind in S with dangling trait references.

Inherent `impl Foo { ... }` blocks are explicitly materialized into S/D by the Phase 1 child-item helpers, but `build_impl_identity_map` drops every `Impl` with `trait_.is_none()`, and Phase 2 only evaluates the resulting impl identity map. That makes `TypeEntry.methods` invisible to the evaluator: adding, removing, or changing an inherent method will never produce a signal.

Enum structural equality only compares the visible variant list and each variant's kind. It ignores `Enum::has_stripped_variants` and every variant's explicit discriminant, so a truncated rustdoc enum or a discriminant-only change can still compare equal and suppress a real drift signal.

P1 correctness: deleted structs/enums/traits are moved to D without repairing their child-id lists. `remove_child_items_from_s` removes fields/variants/trait members from S, but `move_type_to_d` inserts the original parent `Item` into D unchanged, so the parent still points at ids that no longer exist in `d_index`. That leaves D internally malformed for every delete of a struct/enum/trait, and the Phase 1.6 dangling-id check will not catch it because it only walks type references, not structural child lists.

Phase 1.6 treats every non-sentinel referenced Id as valid only if it is present in S or in B's paths. A catalogue-derived Add/Modify item can contain known external references encoded with synthetic Ids in A.paths (for example std::vec::Vec or an external trait impl), but those path entries are not copied into S and this check does not consult A.paths. The referenced synthetic Id then falls through to DanglingId, so valid catalogue entries using external types are rejected before Phase 2.

`format_generic_args` renders both associated-type equality (`Item = Type`) and associated-type constraint (`Item: Bound`) with the same `name:rhs` string. That makes semantically different signatures such as `Iterator<Item = Copy>` and `Iterator<Item: Copy>` compare equal, so Phase 2 can emit a false Match/Blue instead of a mismatch.

Generic type parameters are rendered by their source names during signature comparison. Because `generics_structurally_equal` ignores generic parameter names, otherwise identical APIs that only rename a generic parameter, such as `fn f<T>(x: T)` vs `fn f<U>(x: U)`, will still compare unequal through `format_type` and produce false mismatch signals. Canonicalize generics by parameter position before formatting use sites.

P1: A-sourced `Type::ResolvedPath` ids are never remapped when an Add/Modify item is copied into S. The codec resolves catalogue-local type names to A-side item ids, but this helper only remaps structural child lists and leaves field/generic/return type path ids pointing at the old A id space. For valid declarations like a modified struct field `role: Role` where `Role` remains in S under B's id, Phase 1.6 will either reject with `DanglingId` or, worse, accept the stale id if it happens to collide with an unrelated S item. The A insertion/modify path needs to rewrite local resolved path ids through the S identity map/remap, not rely only on `UNRESOLVED_CRATE_ID`.

Phase 1.6 accepts any referenced Id that happens to exist in S before checking whether that Id came from A's catalogue id space. Add/Modify items keep A-side `ResolvedPath.id` values for local refs, so a reference to a deleted local type can be silently accepted if the A id collides with a retained B/S id. For example, if A assigns `DeleteMe` Id(1) and B/S has `Keep` Id(1), a modified item referring to deleted `DeleteMe` passes this Rule 1 check instead of returning `Phase1Error::DanglingId`, violating the closed-world/dangling-id requirement. Remap A local type refs to S ids, or consult `a_krate.paths` for A-sourced refs before accepting numeric S-id presence.

A-only external type references are accepted by the dangling-id pass using `a_krate.paths`, but the subsequent external-crate rebuild only has access to `b`. For added/modified catalogue items whose fields or bounds reference an external crate that was not present in the baseline, `collect_ext_crate_name_from_path` cannot resolve the non-sentinel A synthetic id, so S is built without the required `external_crates` entry and without a valid path summary for that external id. This violates the Phase 1 per-scope external-crate renumbering contract and can leave the intermediate S graph internally inconsistent for A-added external refs. Pass A's path/external-crate metadata into this rebuild or copy/renumber A external path summaries before constructing S.

Trait impl equality compares method maps, but catalogue-derived trait impls are identity-only and have no encoded methods. Because Phase 2 evaluates impl identities, an achieved declared impl in C will usually have methods while S's A-derived impl has an empty `items` list, causing `S ∩ C` to be classified as a structural mismatch and leaving Add/Modify impls Yellow (or Reference impls Red) indefinitely. Impl equality should not require method-map equality for identity-only declarations, or impls should not be evaluated independently.

Phase 1 only carries impl blocks into S/D when they are reachable through `collect_child_ids`, but `TypeAlias` falls through to `_ => vec![]` and there is no separate pass over standalone `Impl` items. Phase 2 does evaluate impl identities, so trait impls for type aliases, which cannot be attached through a `TypeAlias.impls` field in rustdoc_types, get dropped from S/D and then appear as `CMinusSUnionD` reds even when declared or present in the baseline. Add handling for standalone impl items or an attachment path for aliases before evaluating impls in Phase 2.

Trait method signature comparison canonicalizes only method-local generics (`f.generics`). Methods on generic traits commonly reference the trait's own type parameters, so `trait Repo<T> { fn get(&self) -> T; }` and the same trait with `U` compare as different even though `generics_structurally_equal` intentionally ignores generic parameter names. This can produce false mismatch signals for harmless trait generic renames; include the enclosing trait generics in the canonical map when building trait method signatures.

`move_type_to_d` only moves impls reachable through `collect_child_ids(&item)`, but Phase 1 explicitly inserts orphan impls for items whose rustdoc shape has no `impls` field, especially `TypeAlias`. When such an item is deleted, those orphan impls remain in S as `Reference`; their `impl.for_` still points at the deleted local item, so Phase 1.6 returns `DanglingId` instead of moving the impl to D and producing the expected delete signal. Track orphan impls by their `for_`/`trait_` target and move matching ones during delete.

The per-scope external-crate rebuild creates new `external_crates` maps but never copies the referenced external `ItemSummary` entries from B or A into `state.s_paths`/`state.d_paths` before constructing S and D. Type refs keep their original external synthetic `Id`s, while the resulting `Crate.paths` contains only local top-level summaries, so those ids cannot be resolved against the returned S/D graph and the new crate-id numbering is not actually attached to the referenced paths. Copy referenced external path summaries from the source crate(s) into the scope paths and rewrite their `crate_id` to the new per-scope ids.

Phase 1.45 rewrites every item in `state.s_index` with an A-id to S-id map. Because B and A use independent `rustdoc_types::Id` spaces, this also rewrites B-derived Reference items whose ids happen to equal A ids. That can corrupt B local references and, more importantly, mask a real dangling reference after a delete by changing a B reference to some unrelated retained S item before the Phase 1.6 check runs.

A-sourced external references are only resolved through `extra_a` when the same numeric id is absent from `b.paths`. Since A synthetic external ids can collide with B path ids, an A-added/modified item that references a new external type can be classified using B metadata or skipped entirely, leaving S with missing or wrong `external_crates` / `paths.crate_id` entries.

The A-side id remapper leaves `GenericBound::TraitBound.generic_params` untouched, so local type ids nested under HRTB binders remain in A's id space. The Phase 1.6 collector now recurses into those binders, so valid Add/Modify items with bounds like `for<T: LocalTrait>` can be rejected as `DanglingId` even when `LocalTrait` is present in S.

`identifiers.rs` is 854 lines long, which exceeds the repository's 700-line hard limit for a module. The extra size is mostly from co-locating the large test block with the implementation, so this already violates the stated module-size rule and makes future changes harder to keep coherent.

`ModulePath::from_identifiers` is a new public constructor, but there is no unit test covering its happy path. That leaves a public API path in this module without the required test coverage, so a regression in this constructor would not be caught by the current suite.

`ItemAction` derives `strum::Display`/`EnumString` without a rename policy, so it accepts/emits `Add`, `Modify`, etc. The inherited TDDD-03 catalogue contract uses lowercase tags (`add`, `modify`, `reference`, `delete`), and the existing codec/fixtures use those values. Unless this enum is renamed with `#[strum(serialize_all = "snake_case")]` (and covered by tests), valid catalogue actions will be rejected or re-emitted with the wrong spelling once the v2 codec maps through these domain strings.

`SelfReceiver` currently parses/displays the Rust variant names (`Owned`, `SharedRef`, `ExclusiveRef`), but ADR D8 defines the catalogue values as the receiver tokens `self`, `&self`, and `&mut self`. This makes the domain `FromStr` reject the receiver strings already used in catalogue data and will force every caller to duplicate a custom mapping instead of using the validated domain enum. Add explicit strum serializations for the three variants and tests for the token forms.

`Layer` also derives default strum casing, so `Layer::Domain.to_string()` is `Domain` and parsing `domain` fails. ADR D1 defines the layer axis as the lowercase catalogue values `domain` / `usecase` / `infrastructure`; with the current implementation, valid v2 documents using the specified layer values cannot round-trip through the domain enum. Add the appropriate strum serialization policy and tests for the lowercase spellings.

`ThreeWaySignal` stores the derived kind as a public field, and `ThreeWayEvaluationReport` exposes its `Vec` publicly. That leaves the documented invariant unenforced: external code can construct or mutate a value with mismatched `region`/`signal` or with `Skip` entries inside a report, and `len()`/`has_violations()`/`is_in_progress()` will then report the wrong state. Make the derived state private or validate on construction so the domain type cannot represent an invalid report.

`ThreeWayEvaluationReport::new` does not enforce the module's own invariant that reports contain only non-skip signals. It stores `Skip` entries verbatim, so any caller can construct a report that contradicts the public contract in `port.rs` and leaks skipped items through `iter()`/`len()`. Consider filtering `Skip` values here or making construction fallible/private.

P1: Re-exporting `catalogue_v2::methods::{MethodDeclaration, ParamDeclaration}` here makes `catalogue::MethodDeclaration` look like the old API path, but it is not API-compatible anymore. The V2 constructor now requires `MethodName` / `ParamName` / `TypeRef` and the accessor methods (`name()`, `params()`, `returns()`, `ty()`, etc.) are gone, so downstream code that was compiling against the documented `catalogue::*` surface will break at source level. A plain `pub use` does not provide the promised compatibility shim.

P1: `MethodDeclaration` now derives `Eq` with a public `docs: Option<String>` field, and baseline structural comparison uses full vector equality (`self.methods == other.methods`). That means doc-only differences between two otherwise identical methods will flip `TypeBaselineEntry::structurally_equal()` / `TraitBaselineEntry::structurally_equal()` to `false`, which creates false consistency failures even though docs are non-structural metadata.

`methods_structurally_equal` still treats `ParamDeclaration` as fully equal (`a.params == b.params`), so a harmless parameter binding rename on an unchanged method will now be reported as structural drift. That diverges from the method/function matching logic in `signals.rs` and `consistency.rs`, which intentionally ignores binding names and compares only parameter types.

P1: Re-exporting the V2 `MethodDeclaration`/`ParamDeclaration` here is not source-compatible with the existing `domain::tddd::catalogue::*` callers. The new constructor takes `MethodName`/`ParamName`/`TypeRef` newtypes, so any still-unmigrated call sites that pass `String` arguments will stop compiling. Keep a compatibility shim or finish the downstream rewrites in the same change.

The Phase 2 classification for `S ∩ C` conflicts with the skip rule below it: the table marks structural matches as `🔵`, but the later `skip` rule says `action=Reference` + structural equality should be suppressed instead of reported. As written, the same case is both a blue signal and a skip.

`D3` says this is a `10 領域` table, but the table itself enumerates 11 distinct conditions, and the consequence note repeats the same `10 領域` claim. The count is internally inconsistent; either the heading/bullets need to say 11 or one row needs to be merged.

`transition_methods` is allowed to reference `trait_impls`内の `MethodDeclaration` here, but D10 later removes `methods` from `TraitImplDecl` entirely. That leaves no schema location for the referenced method declarations, so the typestate transition path is internally inconsistent as written.

The Phase 1 `Delete` step removes an item from `S` but never rewrites or rejects surviving references to that item. For any deletion of a still-referenced baseline item, `S` will end up with dangling `Id`s, so the stated fully-resolved `S` cannot actually be constructed.

ADR 1 says the signal evaluator refines action semantics into a "10-region" table, but the referenced ADR 3 D3 defines an 11-region table and repeats that count later. Because ADR 1 explicitly points readers to ADR 3 D3 as the canonical region definition, this cross-reference leaves implementers with conflicting signal-table cardinality.

This assigns implicit Reference resolution for undeclared baseline-derived items to the codec, but ADR 2 D10 keeps the A codec open-world and defers resolution to S construction, while ADR 3 D4 says the signal evaluator dynamically attaches Reference to B. That is a component-boundary contradiction and can lead to implementing baseline-dependent behavior in the catalogue codec.

D is defined as the set of B-derived items declared for deletion, but the pseudocode adds the A/catalogue element to D (`D.add(elt rebuild_id)`) rather than the removed baseline item from S/B. Following this literally makes D contain the delete declaration shape instead of the baseline item, which contradicts the D definition and can corrupt delete progress evaluation and D-specific external_crates rebuilding.

The signal distribution summary omits `S \ C + action=Add`, even though the table immediately above classifies that region as Yellow. ADR 3 defines the Phase 2 table as 11 regions, but ADR 1 still references a '10-region' signal table. That leaves the catalogue ADR pointing at the wrong evaluator contract for action semantics; update the ADR 1 D4/Related text to match ADR 3's 11-region table, or adjust ADR 3 if 10 was intended.

The canonical schema prose introduces `ItemAction`, and ADR 2/3 consume `ItemAction`, but the ADR 1 schema snippets declare `action: TypeAction` for TypeEntry/TraitEntry/FunctionEntry. This is a semantic type-name mismatch in the plan artifacts and will send implementers toward a non-selected action type.

This states `S.external_crates` is the full union of B and A, but the same paragraph says deletes can remove dependencies from S and leave them only in D. A full B∪A union cannot drop a baseline-only dependency used solely by a deleted item, so the S/D construction contract is internally inconsistent and may make Phase 2 compare against the wrong target graph.

D is defined as the set of B-derived items being deleted, but the Delete branch iterates over A and adds `elt` to D. That contradicts the definition and can lose the baseline item's actual structure/references; the branch should make clear that D receives the matched baseline/S item removed by the delete operation, not the catalogue delete declaration itself.

ADR 2 cites its own D6 as the decision establishing '1 catalogue = 1 crate', but D6 is about fixed values for rustdoc fields; the crate-per-catalogue decision is ADR 1 D6. This broken decision reference appears in the codec rationale and rejected alternative text, so readers are directed to the wrong design decision.

D1 says the Layer axis is determined implicitly from catalogue file placement, but D6 defines an explicit `layer: Layer` field inside `CatalogueDocument` and says the document declares its layer.
